channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Shrinking Ambitions on China
item.description: The president came into office planning harsher trade moves on China than on the rest of the world. Here’s why he’s had to scale them back.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: When President Trump tried to force China into making concessions last year by threatening extreme tariffs, the tactic backfired, forcing the United States to pare back its goals.

item.title: Nvidia C.E.O. Jensen Huang Hitches Ride With Trump to China After Last-Minute Invite
item.description: Jensen Huang boarded Air Force One in Alaska, joining a delegation of more than a dozen business leaders accompanying President Trump on his trip to Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jensen Huang, the chief executive of the Silicon Valley chip giant Nvidia, in San Jose, Calif., in March.

item.title: As Becerra Rises in Polls, He Draws Fire for Trying to Direct Interview
item.description: “This is not a ‘gotcha’ piece, right?” the leading Democratic candidate for California governor asked a television reporter before an interview.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:credit: Rich Pedroncelli/Associated Press
item.media:description: Xavier Becerra, the health secretary in the Biden administration, is now the leading Democratic candidate in polls on the California governor’s race.

item.title: Cindy Burbank Wins Senate Primary in Nebraska, but Plans to Drop Out to Help Dan Osborn
item.description: Democrats in Nebraska appear headed toward the Senate matchup they want, while their primary for a key House district was too close to call.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Walker Pickering for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cindy Burbank, pictured during a recent motorcycle ride, won the Democratic nomination for the U.S. Senate in Nebraska, but she said she planned to drop out of the race in an effort to help Dan Osborn, an independent, win instead.

item.title: Nebraska Gov. Jim Pillen to Face Democrat Lynne Walz
item.description: Democrats face an uphill fight as they seek to unseat Gov. Jim Pillen, who is running for another term in the solidly Republican state.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Rebecca S. Gratz/Associated Press
item.media:description: Lynne Walz, a Democrat and former state legislator, is running for governor of Nebraska.

item.title: Trump’s ‘Golden Dome’ Missile Defense Plan Could Cost $1.2 Trillion
item.description: A report from the Congressional Budget Office said that space-based interceptors, which do not currently exist, would probably consume 60 percent of the total cost.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump during an announcement on his plan to develop the “Golden Dome” missile defense program at the White House last May.

item.title: In San Francisco, the Tents of Homeless People Are Disappearing
item.description: Mayor Daniel Lurie and city leaders have enforced bans on camping and moved people off sidewalks to reduce visible homelessness. Critics say too many homeless people are housed in jail.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Daniel Lurie of San Francisco spoke with a city worker in 2025 about cleaning up the Tenderloin neighborhood.

item.title: Two Charged With Trafficking 260 Pounds of Meth to New Jersey
item.description: They face at least 10 years in prison, and the authorities accused one of the men of being associated with the Sinaloa Cartel.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert Frazer, U.S. attorney for the District of New Jersey. He announced the methamphetamine bust on Tuesday.

item.title: Waymo Is Fixing All of Its Self-Driving Taxis to Avoid Floods
item.description: The company will recall its software after one of its robotaxis entered a flooded road during a heavy downpour in San Antonio last month.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Ariana Gomez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Waymo is recalling about 3,800 robotaxis to fix software that could allow the vehicle to “drive into standing water,” according to a letter on the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration’s website.

item.title: Kash Patel Denies Lying and Excessive Drinking in Heated Exchanges With Democrats
item.description: The remarks, at a hearing ostensibly about the coming budget for law enforcement agencies, veered from sedate exchanges about operational matters to ugly personal confrontations.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kash Patel, the director of the F.B.I., sparred with Democratic lawmakers on Tuesday on Capitol Hill.

item.title: Jason Collins, First Active N.B.A. Player to Come Out as Gay, Dies at 47
item.description: His achievements on the court were eclipsed by an essay he wrote in Sports Illustrated in 2013 in which he declared: “I’m a 34-year-old N.B.A. center. I’m Black and I’m gay.”
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:credit: Kathy Kmonicek/Associated Press
item.media:description: Jason Collins in 2014, the year after he announced he was gay in a front-page essay in Sports Illustrated magazine.

item.title: Secret Service Makes Case for Security Funding Amid G.O.P. Unease on Ballroom
item.description: The presidential protection agency detailed how it would use a proposed $1 billion as Republicans asked for more detail and Democrats went on the attack.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Sean Curran, the director of the Secret Service, on Tuesday at the Capitol, where he joined Republican senators at their weekly party luncheon.

item.title: Trump Administration to Tap Longtime ICE Official to Lead Agency
item.description: David Venturella’s appointment is in line with a push by Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, to keep his department under the radar and out of headlines.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration will install David Venturella, a former career agency official who worked at a private prison company, as acting head of Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

item.title: The South Carolina Republican Who Defied Trump on Redistricting
item.description: Politicians are fighting viciously for power, said Shane Massey, leader of the State Senate. “To what end?” he asked, warning of eroding fundamental American values. “I’m voting no.”
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Jeffrey Collins/Associated Press
item.media:description: Shane Massey said on Tuesday that defying President Trump was a political risk he was willing to take.

item.title: U.S. Intelligence Shows Iran Retains Substantial Missile Capabilities
item.description: Secret new assessments say Iran has operational access to 30 of its 33 missile sites along the Strait of Hormuz, suggesting that its military remains far stronger than President Trump has asserted.
item.dc:creator: Adam Entous, Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Missile displays in Tehran in 2024. According to classified U.S. intelligence assessments, Iran retains roughly 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile.

item.title: South Carolina Lawmakers Halt Redistricting Efforts
item.description: Some Republicans had worried that carving up the district held by Representative James E. Clyburn, the state’s only Democratic congressman, could backfire on them.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Allison Joyce/Reuters
item.media:description: In South Carolina, where Republicans have a stronghold in state government, the push to redistrict has not been as full-throated as in other states.

item.title: Trump Says Americans’ Economic Pain Is Not a Consideration for the Iran War
item.description: Asked whether Americans’ financial situation was motivating him to make a deal, President Trump said bluntly, “Not even a little bit.”
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald Trump spoke to reporters as he departed the White House for a trip to China.

item.title: See How Trump Is Renovating the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool
item.description: A rundown of the problems at the pool and what President Trump is trying to do to fix them.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The government set a May 22 deadline for renovations to be complete at the Reflecting Pool.

item.title: How a Hantavirus Cruise Passenger Is Spending His Quarantine in Nebraska
item.description: Jake Rosmarin, a travel influencer from Boston, is one of 15 passengers from the virus-stricken cruise ship who are being monitored at the National Quarantine Unit in Nebraska.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao

item.title: Florida Plans to Close ‘Alligator Alcatraz,’ Vendors Are Reportedly Told
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security has determined the state-run immigration detention facility is too expensive, and some private vendors have struggled to front costs.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Rebecca Blackwell/Associated Press
item.media:description: Florida intends to shut down a high-profile immigration detention center that opened last summer in the Everglades.

item.title: Obama Enters Texas Senate Fight With Support for Talarico
item.description: The former president, who has looked to elevate a new generation of Democrats, met with James Talarico and a candidate for governor, Gina Hinojosa, who are trying to flip Texas.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Joel Angel Juarez
item.media:description: Texas gubernatorial candidate Gina Hinojosa (D-TX), Texas Senate candidate James Talarico (D-TX) and Former President Barack Obama meet patrons at the restaurant Taco Joint on the campaign trail on Tuesday in Austin.

item.title: A Dying Son, His Detained Parents and a Race to Reunite Before the End
item.description: From his hospital bed in Chicago, Kevin Gonzalez had a final wish to see his parents. But they were in immigration detention in Arizona after illegally crossing to see their son.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy

item.title: Man Who Stole Unreleased Beyoncé Music Is Sentenced to 5 Years
item.description: The theft occurred on July 8, 2025, when members of the singer’s team reported a vehicle break-in ahead of her “Cowboy Carter” tour dates in Atlanta.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: A man accused of stealing hard drives containing unreleased music by Beyoncé pleaded guilty on Tuesday in Atlanta, officials said.

item.title: Alabama Governor Sets New Primary Elections for Four House Seats
item.description: Gov. Kay Ivey scheduled special House primaries for August after the Supreme Court cleared a path for Alabama to use a 2023 congressional map with one majority-Black district.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Julie Bennett/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Kay Ivey of Alabama set a special election on Tuesday for four House races.

item.title: Texas Murder Suspect Lee Gilley Flees to Italy, Setting Up Possible Extradition Fight
item.description: The case could test the limits of a decades-old extradition treaty, because Italy bars extradition in cases where a defendant could face the death penalty.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Conley/Houston Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Lee Gilley was charged with capital murder in Texas in October 2024, days after he called the police to say that his wife was unresponsive. Federal authorities say he fled to Italy ahead of a May trial date.

item.title: Trump and Xi Enter China Summit Distracted by Iran War
item.description: The war in Iran has cast a shadow of uncertainty on both superpowers, dimming early hopes that they could begin to address the larger issues that have frayed their relationship.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with President Xi Jinping of China in Busan, South Korea, last year. The two leaders are set to meet in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Betty Broderick, Whose Murder Trial Was Grist for TV Movies, Dies at 78
item.description: She killed her ex-husband and his new wife after what she described as years of psychological abuse. The crime inspired TV adaptations, a podcast and at least five books.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:credit: San Diego Union-Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Betty Broderick at the San Diego County Courthouse in 1991, during her second trial for the murder of her ex-husband and his new wife.

item.title: Reflecting Pool Repairs Appear Uneven and Behind Schedule, Officials Say
item.description: Work on the iconic National Mall basin appears behind schedule. President Trump initially said he had picked the contractor, then said he did not know it.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: About 35 percent of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool’s surface had been fully coated as of Sunday, according to a government document. The repair work is supposed to be done by May 22.

item.title: Marty Makary, Trump’s F.D.A. Commissioner, Resigns After Weeks of Pressure
item.description: The agency’s top food official will step in to the role after Dr. Makary privately said he opposed the administration decision to approve flavored vapes.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Marty Makary at times tried to advance his own principles alongside the administration’s goals, which at times were at odds with the powerful food, tobacco and pharmaceutical industries.

item.title: Hegseth to Accompany Trump on Trip to China
item.description: Defense secretaries have previously traveled with presidents on overseas trips, to provide advice and represent the U.S. military.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth arriving to testify before the Senate Appropriations subcommittee on defense.

item.title: Student Is Fatally Stabbed in University of Washington Housing Complex
item.description: Police officers responding to a call found the student dead in the laundry room of an off-campus residential building. Investigators are now searching for a suspect.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Seattle Police Department
item.media:description: Police officers are hunting for the person responsible for the killing in a building less than a mile from campus.

item.title: Mysterious Meddling in Democratic Primaries Has G.O.P. Fingerprints
item.description: A new super PAC with ties to Republicans is intervening in Democratic primaries, in one instance helping a sex therapist in Texas accused of antisemitism.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters waiting in line to cast ballots in the Texas primary in Austin in March 3.

item.title: Raffensperger Campaign Event at Georgia Airport Is Disrupted by ‘Active Threat,’ Bomb Squad on Site
item.description: A four-page ‘manifesto’ targeted Brad Raffensperger, the Republican secretary of state, and featured his photo with the word “Boom” written across his forehead, his campaign said.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Richard Fausset and Allison Braden
item.media:credit: Elijah Nouvelage/Getty Images
item.media:description: Brad Raffensperger’s campaign announced shortly after the incident that his event, at a local airport, had faced an “active threat.”

item.title: Person Fatally Struck by Frontier Airlines Jet at Denver Airport Is Identified
item.description: The individual, a 41-year-old man, made it through several layers of security at the airport on Friday minutes before being struck by a Frontier Airlines jet, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: David Zalubowski/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Frontier Airlines jet that fatally struck a person was parked at the airline’s technical operations center north of Denver International Airport on Saturday.

item.title: Pentagon Puts Iran War Cost at $29 Billion as Hegseth Deflects on Funding
item.description: The estimate from the Defense Department’s top budget official was $4 billion higher than two weeks ago. Secretary Pete Hegseth declined to say when the administration would request extra money for the Iran conflict.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Jay Hurst, the Pentagon comptroller; Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth; and Gen. Dan Caine testified about the Defense Department’s budget request on Tuesday.

item.title: Jen Kiggans, Virginia Republican, Faces Uproar Over Radio Host’s Racist Remark About Jeffries
item.description: Representative Jen Kiggans, a Republican in a tough re-election fight, faced criticism after she agreed with comments by a local radio host that included a racist line about Hakeem Jeffries, the Democratic minority leader.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Jen Kiggans of Virginia, a Republican, said on Monday night that her remarks had been misconstrued by Democrats.

item.title: Justice Department Charges Companies in Baltimore Bridge Collapse
item.description: The criminal charges are the latest legal fallout after the 2024 crash, which killed six men who were working on the Key Bridge.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times
item.media:description: A cargo ship hit the Francis Scott Key Bridge in Baltimore in 2024, leading to the deaths of six people.

item.title: California Gubernatorial Candidate Steve Hilton Mocked for Calling Del Taco Order a ‘Street Taco’
item.description: Steve Hilton, a Republican front-runner for governor, posted a video of his Del Taco order. Social media users accused him of misunderstanding the state’s Mexican food culture.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Rene Ray De La Cruz/Daily Press/USA Today, via Imagn Images, via Reuters
item.media:description: After a recent event in Barstow, Calif., Republican candidate Steve Hilton stopped by the oldest operating Del Taco in the country.

item.title: Oregon Prepares for a Challenging Summer of Water Shortages and High Fire Risk
item.description: After a warm winter that brought more rain than snow, the state’s snowpack was the lowest it has ever been.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff, Anna Griffin and Loren Elliott
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times

item.title: China Sought Access to Anthropic’s Newest A.I. The Answer Was No.
item.description: The latest artificial intelligence models from Anthropic and OpenAI are extending the United States’ lead over China and intensifying the rivalry between the countries.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz, Julian E. Barnes, Sheera Frenkel and Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Marissa Leshnov for The New York Times
item.media:description: A growing number of national security officials and analysts have begun to liken the intensifying competition between China and the United States over artificial intelligence to the Cold War’s nuclear arms race.

item.title: What to Watch in Tuesday’s Primaries in Nebraska and West Virginia
item.description: Democrats will choose a candidate in a House battleground in Nebraska that could decide the fate of Omaha’s “blue dot” vote.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: Some people are concerned that a quirk in how Nebraska allocates its Electoral College votes, known as the “blue dot" system, could be abolished by Republicans.

item.title: Former Congressman Barney Frank, Now in Hospice, Offers Democrats Frank Words of Advice
item.description: Mr. Frank speaks about the missteps of the Democratic Party and his hope for its future. “Frankly, if I weren’t dying, people wouldn’t be paying as much attention.”
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:credit: Greg Miller for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former Representative Barney Frank in Ogunquit, Maine, in 2017. Mr. Frank, who has congestive heart failure, is speaking out on the future of the Democratic Party.

item.title: Texas Rainbow Crosswalk Ban Leads San Antonio to Paint Sidewalks Instead
item.description: After the state ordered the removal of rainbow crosswalks, San Antonio used colorful sidewalks to keep public pride symbols alive in the city.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Christopher Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The rainbows on crosswalks began disappearing after the state ordered all Texas cities to remove the colorful crosswalks and similar markings, citing safety issues and opposition to political messaging.
